turns.content: I've been reading the docs on collections and I keep getting stuck on how to use BinaryHeap as a priority queue. I know it's a max-heap by default, but I'm not sure if there's a better way to get a min-heap without just flipping my data later.

The core reason BinaryHeap exists is that it provides O(1) access to the maximum element and efficient insertion/removal, which standard Vecs cannot do. For a max-heap, you simply pass BinaryHeap<T> directly because its internal ordering guarantees the largest item is at index zero. To get a min-heap without reversing your entire collection upfront, you use the Reverse trait by wrapping T in Reverse<T>, so the smallest element becomes the maximum of that wrapper type and sits at the head.
That makes sense for ordering but what about checking if an element exists or getting its value? I'm confused about peek versus pop, especially since I don't want to lose my data just to look at it.

The distinction is critical because BinaryHeap does not support random access; there is no get method. Peek returns a mutable reference to the top element so you can inspect or update it without removing it from the heap, whereas pop removes and discards the maximum item permanently. If you need both inspection and removal later, peek first to read the value, then call pop if you want to extract it.
Okay, I see that now but what about initializing a heap from an existing vector? Do I have to manually build it or does BinaryHeap do something special?

When you construct a BinaryHeap from a Vec using push methods, the heap property is not guaranteed until every insertion completes. However, if you call heapify on that collection after filling it, Rust will perform a single O(n) restructure operation to satisfy the binary heap invariant in linear time rather than doing n insertions.
That's helpful but I'm still confused about how exactly Reverse<T> works with generics. Can't I just use min_heap or something?

There is no separate min_heap type in the standard library; BinaryHeap handles both max and min by leveraging the generic constraints on the comparison function. By default, it uses T::cmp which orders from smallest to largest, making the largest element the root for a max-heap. When you wrap T with Reverse<T>, the internal logic effectively flips the order so that the smallest original value becomes the new maximum of the wrapper type.

I was thinking I could just use a regular Vec with sort to make a priority queue, but I heard there's a better way using collections::BinaryHeap. Since it's a max-heap by default, how do I actually implement a min-priority queue? Do I need to write custom comparison logic or is there a trait I can just apply like Reverse?

You are right that BinaryHeap is the idiomatic choice over sorting for priority queues because it offers O(1) push and pop operations instead of the O(n log n) cost of repeated sorts. For a min-heap, you don't need custom logic; you simply wrap your generic type T in Reverse<T>. This implements the necessary ordering automatically so BinaryHeap::push works as expected for minimum values without needing to write custom comparators or traits yourself.

Okay, that sounds efficient but I'm worried about performance if I use Reverse. Does wrapping everything in Reverse add significant overhead? And what happens if my priority queue gets very large—does the heap structure stay balanced even with thousands of elements?

Reverse<T> is zero-copy and has no runtime overhead since it's just a generic wrapper that changes the comparison order; the internal BinaryHeap structure remains balanced regardless of size. The heap property is maintained automatically by BinaryHeap's push, pop, and peek methods, so even with thousands of elements you won't see performance degradation because there are no manual rebalancing steps required.

That makes sense about the overhead but I still need to know how to initialize this from existing data. If I have a Vec already sorted by min value, can I just convert it directly into BinaryHeap? Or do I need to call heapify or something else first?

You don't need any special function like heapify; you simply pass your Vec directly to BinaryHeap::from_iter which will automatically build the balanced tree structure in O(n) time. This is much faster than pushing elements one by one, so converting a sorted vec into a BinaryHeap is both idiomatic and efficient for large datasets.

Wow, I didn't realize BinaryHeap could do that automatically with from_iter! That clears up the initialization confusion completely. So my min-heap implementation just needs Reverse<Vec<i32>> + BinaryHeap::from_iter and then standard push/pop calls? This feels like it's finally solving the problem elegantly instead of reinventing sorting logic.

Exactly, that is the idiomatic approach you should adopt. You can now confidently use collections::BinaryHeap with Reverse for min-priority queues without worrying about manual comparisons or inefficient rebuilding methods.

Hey, I'm getting this error: thread 'main' panicked at 'attempt to push into a full BinaryHeap', because it seems like the heap is already full. I tried using a regular Vec but that doesn't seem right for my priority queue logic.

That's exactly what happens with a standard BinaryHeap when you try to push past its capacity limit, so you definitely need the collections crate instead. The key distinction is that BinaryHeap implements Drop and pop operations efficiently by default, but if you want a min-heap since your priority queue needs smallest tasks first, you should use Reverse<T> on the heap type itself to flip the ordering.

Oh, I thought BinaryHeap was just like Vec with some extra magic, so maybe I could just add a capacity field. But wait, if I'm building this from scratch, how do I initialize it properly? Does the heapify function work on an empty collection?

You're right to be careful there, because BinaryHeap doesn't support capacity limits like Vec does and always resizes as needed. For initialization from a vector of tasks, you should use the heapify_from method on the BinaryHeap type itself rather than trying to set a fixed size upfront.
